jokes.text: They'll find me in the spring buried in the snow like one of those fucking dead moose.
jokes.setup: Discussion about cold weather in Montana being invigorating
jokes.punchline: Larry's morbid, specific vision of his frozen corpse compared to wildlife
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry neurosis - takes reasonable concern and spirals into vivid, specific doom scenario. Character-specific paranoia delivered with casual profanity.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: They'll find me in the spring buried in the snow like one of those fucking dead moose.

jokes.text: No, you didn't. You already booked reservations at my favorite restaurant. You're surprising me with a cake-- Chocolate hazelnut.
jokes.setup: Artie pretends he forgot Larry's birthday
jokes.punchline: Larry lists all the things Artie already arranged, including specific cake flavor
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - shows Larry's need to control even his own surprises and Artie's resigned competence

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, you didn't. You already booked reservations at my favorite restaurant. You're surprising me with a cake-- Chocolate hazelnut.

jokes.text: Well, if we had better numbers, we wouldn't need to go on the road to get better numbers.
jokes.setup: Artie says they need better numbers to go to Hawaii
jokes.punchline: Larry points out the circular logic trap they're in
jokes.explanation: Smart structural joke about show business catch-22s. Economical and character-appropriate cynicism.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, if we had better numbers, we wouldn't need to go on the road to get better numbers.

jokes.text: How thoughtful of me.
jokes.setup: Hank thanks Larry for remembering his birthday and booking restaurant
jokes.punchline: Larry's dry acknowledgment that he's praising himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - three words that capture Larry's self-awareness and mild narcissism. Classic Shandling delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: How thoughtful of me.

jokes.text: Oh, you can't go sneaking up on people like that! This is the copy room. I came in to make a copy.
jokes.setup: Beverly is caught doing something suspicious in copy room
jokes.punchline: Her over-defensive explanation states the obvious
jokes.explanation: Functional character moment showing Beverly's guilt, but fairly standard 'caught in the act' comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Oh, you can't go sneaking up on people like that! This is the copy room. I came in to make a copy.

jokes.text: Are you smoking pot? No. I'm just making a copy of something.
jokes.setup: Mary Lou assumes Beverly's suspicious behavior means drugs
jokes.punchline: Beverly's innocent response contrasts with her actual guilty secret
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection - audience thinks one kind of illicit activity, reality is different but equally problematic.

dialogue.speaker: Mary Lou
dialogue.line: Are you smoking pot?

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: No. I'm just making a copy of something.

jokes.text: Next time he asks me out, I'm gonna say yes.
jokes.setup: Mary Lou sees Jake the grip's high salary in the budget
jokes.punchline: Her immediate mercenary romantic calculation
jokes.explanation: Sharp character moment - instant shift from romance to economics. Economical and cynically funny.

dialogue.speaker: Mary Lou
dialogue.line: Next time he asks me out, I'm gonna say yes.

jokes.text: How can you make that much money for just standing around gossiping? Oh, don't you just hate 'em? They have nothing better to do than just snoop in other peoples' business.
jokes.setup: Beverly complains about hair and makeup gossiping
jokes.punchline: The irony that she's literally snooping and gossiping right now
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic hypocrisy - Beverly criticizes others for exactly what she's doing. Self-aware writing that trusts the audience to get the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: How can you make that much money for just standing around gossiping? Oh, don't you just hate 'em? They have nothing better to do than just snoop in other peoples' business.

jokes.text: It was eva gabor... It was her first orgasm, and she just couldn't thank me enough.
jokes.setup: Hank is telling a story (presumably fabricated)
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous claim about Eva Gabor's sexual inexperience
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank character moment - delusional sexual braggadocio with specific celebrity name-dropping. Tambor's character speciality.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It was eva gabor... It was her first orgasm, and she just couldn't thank me enough.

jokes.text: Zsa Zsa was at the wheel. Oh, mama! And I was-- as you might say-- I was working under the hood.
jokes.setup: Continuing the Eva Gabor car story
jokes.punchline: Double entendre about car maintenance and oral sex
jokes.explanation: Standard double entendre but delivered with perfect Hank smugness and self-satisfaction

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Zsa Zsa was at the wheel. Oh, mama! And I was-- as you might say-- I was working under the hood.

jokes.text: It's a wallet. It's got a fish on it. Oh, that's a nice one. You like it? Yeah. Ok. You--it's yours. Really? 'Cause I need a new wallet.
jokes.setup: Larry gives Hank a birthday gift
jokes.punchline: Awkward reversal where Larry takes it back because Hank needs it
jokes.explanation: Beautifully uncomfortable moment - Larry's cheap gift becomes even cheaper when he gives it away. Perfect cringe setup and payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: It's a wallet. It's got a fish on it.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, that's a nice one.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You like it?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Ok. You--it's yours.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: 'Cause I need a new wallet.

jokes.text: No, no, that's 3 o'clock. 12 o'clock! No, this is 12 o'clock. No, you're 3. I'm 12 o'clock.
jokes.setup: Trying to discreetly point out Ellen DeGeneres's location
jokes.punchline: Completely confused directional argument that defeats the purpose
jokes.explanation: Classic comedy confusion - simple task becomes impossible through overthinking. Visual comedy we can imagine from dialogue.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, no, that's 3 o'clock. 12 o'clock!

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: No, this is 12 o'clock.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, you're 3. I'm 12 o'clock.

jokes.text: That lady is a lightning rod of sexual controversy. I smell ratings.
jokes.setup: Spotting Ellen DeGeneres at the restaurant
jokes.punchline: Artie's immediate reduction of her to commercial opportunity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie line - colorful metaphor followed by crass commercial instinct. 'Lightning rod of sexual controversy' is great phrase.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: That lady is a lightning rod of sexual controversy. I smell ratings.

jokes.text: Are you our waiter? I am your waiter. Gee, I thought things were going so well.
jokes.setup: Larry approaches Ellen's table
jokes.punchline: Ellen's dry comment about Larry needing a second job
jokes.explanation: Sharp comeback - Ellen immediately gets a dig in about Larry's career status. Good setup/punchline structure.

dialogue.speaker: Ellen
dialogue.line: Are you our waiter?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I am your waiter.

dialogue.speaker: Ellen
dialogue.line: Gee, I thought things were going so well.

jokes.text: Thursday we've got superman, hercules, and dr. Quinn medicine woman. Wow. Well, then, some other time. No, no, hercules can go fuck himself.
jokes.setup: Larry lists impressive Thursday guests, Ellen politely backs off
jokes.punchline: Larry's sudden vulgarity dismissing Hercules
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from professional to profane. 'Hercules can go fuck himself' is perfectly absurd and quotable. Great character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Thursday we've got superman, hercules, and dr. Quinn medicine woman.

dialogue.speaker: Ellen
dialogue.line: Wow. Well, then, some other time.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, no, hercules can go fuck himself.

jokes.text: I'll get some rolls and I'll come back. Get bread rolls. Whatever you want. Yeah, sourdough is good. You got it. I--I may not be back for a couple of days.
jokes.setup: Larry maintaining waiter charade
jokes.punchline: Casually mentioning he might abandon his waiter duties for days
jokes.explanation: Absurd commitment to the bit - Larry treats fake waiter job like real employment with vacation days. Good character logic.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'll get some rolls and I'll come back. Get bread rolls. Whatever you want. Yeah, sourdough is good. You got it. I--I may not be back for a couple of days.

jokes.text: Oh, for god sakes. How come every time you see 2 women together, you automatically think they're gay? We're together. Does that mean we're gay?! No, but the night's still young.
jokes.setup: Larry defends against assumption about Ellen and her friend
jokes.punchline: Hank's implication about their own evening plans
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup and punchline - Larry's righteous defense gets punctured by Hank's suggestive comeback. Great timing and character work.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, for god sakes. How come every time you see 2 women together, you automatically think they're gay? We're together. Does that mean we're gay?!

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: No, but the night's still young.

jokes.text: You've got all of Hank's lines under the name 'Nurse libido.' That's his name in the sketch. Yeah, haven't I told you that you never write Hank's character name on the card? You have to write 'hank,' or he won't read it.
jokes.setup: Confusion about cue cards for Hank
jokes.punchline: Hank's ego requires his real name, not character name
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character detail - reveals Hank's narcissism through specific, believable behind-scenes dysfunction. Very industry-specific humor.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: You've got all of Hank's lines under the name 'Nurse libido.' That's his name in the sketch.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Yeah, haven't I told you that you never write Hank's character name on the card? You have to write 'hank,' or he won't read it.

jokes.text: Just because you make more money than me doesn't mean you're my boss. Are you nuts? I worked for bob hope. You should be kissing my ass that I even print your shit.
jokes.setup: Workplace hierarchy dispute
jokes.punchline: Phil's appeal to his Bob Hope credentials for dominance
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how Hollywood credentials become weapons. Bob Hope name-drop establishes Phil's old-school status.

dialogue.speaker: Writer
dialogue.line: Just because you make more money than me doesn't mean you're my boss.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Are you nuts? I worked for bob hope. You should be kissing my ass that I even print your shit.

jokes.text: Then why do they pay me 1,000 times what you make, huh, jerkby? It's 5 times.
jokes.setup: Writer boasts about salary superiority
jokes.punchline: Phil's correction deflates the insult with specific math
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation - Phil's precise correction undercuts the writer's hyperbole. Great economy and character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Writer
dialogue.line: Then why do they pay me 1,000 times what you make, huh, jerkby?

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: It's 5 times.

jokes.text: Christ, this is the budget. Where did you get this? From my ass!
jokes.setup: Phil discovers the leaked budget
jokes.punchline: Writer's crude non-answer escalates the tension
jokes.explanation: Crude escalation that relies on shock value rather than wit. Functional for showing workplace tension but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Christ, this is the budget. Where did you get this?

dialogue.speaker: Writer
dialogue.line: From my ass!

jokes.text: Well, listen, I believe I know more about homosexuality than you two. After all, I'm the one with the gay assistant.
jokes.setup: Artie establishing his credentials on homosexuality
jokes.punchline: His logic that having a gay assistant makes him an expert
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Artie's false logic about expertise through proximity. Reveals his shallow thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Well, listen, I believe I know more about homosexuality than you two. After all, I'm the one with the gay assistant.

jokes.text: And that's--that's why I know she's a doughnut humper. Bumper. That's why I know she's a doughnut bumper.
jokes.setup: Artie trying to use crude slang
jokes.punchline: His immediate self-correction to slightly different crude slang
jokes.explanation: Artie's attempt at crude terminology gets confused. Shows his discomfort with the slang he's trying to use.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: And that's--that's why I know she's a doughnut humper. Bumper. That's why I know she's a doughnut bumper.

jokes.text: They would let us do the fucking show from between Anna nicole smith's titties.
jokes.setup: Larry imagining network executives being so happy they'd let them broadcast from anywhere
jokes.punchline: Extremely specific and absurd location involving Anna Nicole Smith
jokes.explanation: Memorably crude and specific escalation. Very quotable absurdist image, though relies somewhat on shock value.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: They would let us do the fucking show from between Anna nicole smith's titties.

jokes.text: Jesus, Hank is the most overpaid man in show business.
jokes.setup: Jake looking at the budget information
jokes.punchline: His assessment of Hank's compensation relative to contribution
jokes.explanation: Direct assessment that rings true to audience. Economy of language makes it quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jake
dialogue.line: Jesus, Hank is the most overpaid man in show business.

jokes.text: Oh, those jerks. They spelled my name wrong. Oh, that--Trollop! Yeah, he is.

jokes.punchline: Brian's agreement that the writer is indeed a trollop
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay - Brian treats the misspelling as accurate description. Mild but functional.

dialogue.speaker: Writer
dialogue.line: Oh, those jerks. They spelled my name wrong. Oh, that--Trollop!

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: Yeah, he is.

jokes.text: I--I make $5.00 more a week than you make? Yeah. I mean, don't you think you should make a little bit more than me? Yeah, but I only make $5.00 more than you.
jokes.setup: Brian and Beverly comparing their tiny salary difference
jokes.punchline: Brian's deflated realization of how small his advantage is
jokes.explanation: Great workplace comedy - the petty salary differences that feel huge until examined. Good character work.

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: I--I make $5.00 more a week than you make?

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: I mean, don't you think you should make a little bit more than me?

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Yeah, but I only make $5.00 more than you.

jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd and disgusting tasks he performs for Hank
jokes.explanation: Escalating absurdity that reveals the depths of assistant duties in Hollywood. Highly quotable and perfectly ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Brian
dialogue.line: Have you ever waxed Larry's back? Have you ever poked around in great dane shit looking for a ring?

jokes.text: I mean, john lithgow isn't really from outer space. Well, you don't know him as well as I do. I think there's a good chance he is.
jokes.setup: Ellen making point about actors playing different roles
jokes.punchline: Larry's deadpan suggestion that Lithgow might actually be alien
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan callback to Lithgow's alien roles. Larry's delivery suggests he's genuinely suspicious. Great timing.

dialogue.speaker: Ellen
dialogue.line: I mean, john lithgow isn't really from outer space.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, you don't know him as well as I do. I think there's a good chance he is.

jokes.text: All that publicity and we're still getting our ass kicked by the nanny. That hurts.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Ellen's publicity
jokes.punchline: Larry's wounded pride about losing to a CBS sitcom
jokes.explanation: Good industry-specific humor about ratings competition. Larry's ego taking hits from unexpected sources.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: All that publicity and we're still getting our ass kicked by the nanny. That hurts.

jokes.text: And then I kiss you. And then--then you can decide. That could be funny. Yeah. Yeah. Like, um... Like this.
jokes.setup: Ellen suggesting a bit for the show involving a kiss
jokes.punchline: The immediate transition to actually doing it
jokes.explanation: Awkward escalation from theoretical to actual. Sets up the major cringe sequence that follows.

dialogue.speaker: Ellen
dialogue.line: And then I kiss you. And then--then you can decide. That could be funny.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Yeah. Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Ellen
dialogue.line: Like, um... Like this.

jokes.text: [Ellen kisses Larry]
jokes.setup: Ellen suggesting they test her sexuality by kissing Larry
jokes.punchline: The actual kiss happening

jokes.text: God, what a surprise. Yeah. That is--Jesus. I mean, where did you learn all that? Oh, camp.
jokes.setup: Larry impressed by Ellen's kissing
jokes.punchline: Ellen's casual 'camp' explanation
jokes.explanation: Perfect punchline - 'camp' works on multiple levels (summer camp vs. gay culture). Economical and brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: God, what a surprise. Yeah. That is--Jesus. I mean, where did you learn all that?

dialogue.speaker: Ellen
dialogue.line: Oh, camp.

jokes.text: Tonight, Ellen has a secret, and Larry's got it!
jokes.setup: Stevie planning promotional strategy
jokes.punchline: His cheesy promo copy that sounds sexual

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Tonight, Ellen has a secret, and Larry's got it!

jokes.text: Unless she had sex with her mother. Now, that would be a great cue. 'Tonight on Larry Sanders, Ellen sleeps with her mother! It's a family affair!'
jokes.setup: Stevie dismissing Ellen's mother story
jokes.punchline: His increasingly inappropriate promotional ideas
jokes.explanation: Escalating absurdity showing agent's complete lack of boundaries. Somewhat shock-value dependent but character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Unless she had sex with her mother. Now, that would be a great cue. 'Tonight on Larry Sanders, Ellen sleeps with her mother! It's a family affair!'

jokes.text: What if she's not a lesbian? Did you guys ever think of that? Oh, man. I--she's a lesbian, I can tell. I've had sex with a lesbian.

jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd Artie logic delivered with complete confidence. Character-specific sexual bravado.

jokes.text: I need to make more money. Phil! What? Oh, I'm sorry. Did I just say that out loud? I've been saying it over and over in my head, and I guess one just slipped out.

jokes.punchline: His realization he vocalized his private thoughts
jokes.explanation: Great psychological comedy - the budget leak has Phil obsessed. Relatable internal thought becoming external.

dialogue.line: I need to make more money.
Phil!

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: What? Oh, I'm sorry. Did I just say that out loud? I've been saying it over and over in my head, and I guess one just slipped out.

jokes.text: This is our opportunity here to kick leno's fucking ass!
jokes.setup: Stevie's excitement about the Ellen opportunity

jokes.explanation: Standard agent aggression and competition. Character-appropriate but not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: This is our opportunity here to kick leno's fucking ass!

jokes.text: I recently caught an old cosby rerun, and suddenly my character's a black, 50-year-old gynecologist, and I just--no more.
jokes.setup: Larry explaining how TV influences his work
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous specific example of how he supposedly absorbs influences
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist escalation. Specific, unexpected, and perfectly captures Larry's pretentious self-importance. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I recently caught an old cosby rerun, and suddenly my character's a black, 50-year-old gynecologist, and I just--no more.

jokes.punchline: His admission of his own verbal incompetence
jokes.explanation: Perfect meta-commentary on his own fumbling. Self-aware and honest about his failure to communicate. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'm trying to scramble some of these words around to form a sentence.

jokes.text: Would the character of Ellen ever sleep with a man? Well, sure, if he were feminine enough.
jokes.setup: Larry finally manages to ask his question
jokes.punchline: Ellen's perfect comeback that turns it back on Larry
jokes.explanation: Perfect punchline - Ellen turns Larry's question into an insult about his masculinity. Brilliant structure, economy, and timing. Near-perfect joke.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Would the character of Ellen ever sleep with a man?

dialogue.speaker: Ellen
dialogue.line: Well, sure, if he were feminine enough.

jokes.text: I didn't sleep with him. Am--am--am I the only host you've slept with? Am I the only guest you've slept with?
jokes.setup: Ellen mentions not sleeping with Letterman
jokes.punchline: Larry's increasingly desperate attempts to confirm their sexual encounter
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Larry's desperation. Shows his ego needing validation through sexual conquest comparison.

dialogue.speaker: Ellen
dialogue.line: I didn't sleep with him.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Am--am--am I the only host you've slept with? Am I the only guest you've slept with?

jokes.text: Very, very, very, funny. I not only peed me pants, I shat them.
jokes.setup: Hank reacting to the interview tension
jokes.punchline: His crude escalation of bathroom humor
jokes.explanation: Standard Hank crudeness. Character-appropriate but relies on scatological humor without much wit.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Very, very, very, funny. I not only peed me pants, I shat them.

jokes.text: So, I guess I should say that I, last night, slept with Larry Sanders.
jokes.setup: Ellen's serious moment about privacy vs. public life
jokes.punchline: Her shocking revelation that completely changes the dynamic
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic escalation and misdirection. Takes sincere moment and explodes it with shocking revelation. Masterful structure and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Ellen
dialogue.line: So, I guess I should say that I, last night, slept with Larry Sanders.

jokes.text: What's odd, though, it seemed--you seemed very uncomfortable with me on top, and I thought, 'Why is it?' But then again, you seemed uncomfortable with me on the bottom, too.
jokes.setup: Ellen continuing her fictional sexual encounter story
jokes.punchline: Her detailed critique of Larry's performance issues
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of the fake sexual encounter. Ellen's detailed performance review is both funny and devastating to Larry's ego.

dialogue.speaker: Ellen
dialogue.line: What's odd, though, it seemed--you seemed very uncomfortable with me on top, and I thought, 'Why is it?' But then again, you seemed uncomfortable with me on the bottom, too.

jokes.text: He must have--he must have asked me 3 times, 'How am I doing?' And so, uh... That was, uh--that was sweet of you.
jokes.setup: Ellen continuing her performance critique
jokes.punchline: Larry's insecure need for sexual validation presented as 'sweet'
jokes.explanation: Perfect character assassination disguised as compliment. Captures Larry's neurotic need for approval in sexual context. Devastating and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Ellen
dialogue.line: He must have--he must have asked me 3 times, 'How am I doing?' And so, uh... That was, uh--that was sweet of you.

jokes.text: I woke up this morning thinking that there was something very amazing. Thing's that I'd never felt before, and I realized I was laying on your hairbrush at one point.
jokes.setup: Ellen describing amazing feelings from their encounter
jokes.punchline: Reveals it was just Larry's hairbrush causing the sensation
jokes.explanation: Masterful misdirection - builds up romantic expectation then deflates it completely. Perfect structure and devastating punchline about Larry's inadequacy.

dialogue.speaker: Ellen
dialogue.line: I woke up this morning thinking that there was something very amazing. Thing's that I'd never felt before, and I realized I was laying on your hairbrush at one point.

jokes.text: I guess we'll be doing the show from Bakersfield now.
jokes.setup: Larry's complete humiliation on national television
jokes.punchline: His resigned acceptance that his career is over, referencing earlier Hawaii dreams
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback and character moment. Larry's deadpan acceptance of career destruction, with Bakersfield representing rock bottom vs. Hawaii dreams. Brilliant economy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I guess we'll be doing the show from Bakersfield now.
